---
title: ""
author: "Giorgio Arcara"
date: ""
format:
  beamer:
    theme: metropolis
    keep-tex: false
incremental: false
header-includes:
  \setbeamerfont{title}{size=\large}
  \usepackage{hyperref}
  \setbeamertemplate{footline}[frame number]
  \usepackage{tikz}
  \usetikzlibrary{arrows.meta, positioning, shapes.geometric}
  \usetikzlibrary{overlay-beamer-styles}
---

```{=latex}
\title{{\fontsize{15pt}{16pt}\selectfont Applied Psychometrics}:\\ studying subclinical psychopathologies\\ \vspace{1.5em} \raggedright 
\emph{10.3 - Bayesian Reasoning in Clinical Testing}}
\author{Giorgio Arcara,\\ University of Padua \\ IRCCS San Camillo, Venice}

\titlegraphic{
\vspace*{7cm}
\includegraphics[scale=0.15]{Figures/LogoSanCamilloIRCCS_Unipd_alpha.png}
\begin{center}
\vspace{-2.2em}
\includegraphics[scale=0.15]{Figures/CC_license_3_0.png}
\end{center}
}
\date{2026}
\maketitle
```

## A Common Confusion

When a patient scores exceed a threshold, it is tempting to say:

\begin{center}
\large ``The probability that this person has the condition is 85\%''\\
\small \textit{(if sensitivity = 0.85)}
\end{center}

\pause
\vfill

This is \textbf{wrong}.

\vfill

Sensitivity tells us:
$$P(\text{positive test} \mid \text{condition present}) = 0.85$$

What we \emph{want} is:
$$P(\text{condition present} \mid \text{positive test}) = \;?$$

## From PPV to Bayes Theorem

$$P(A \mid B) \neq P(B \mid A)$$

\vfill
```{=latex}
\begin{columns}
\column{0.5\textwidth}
\vspace{0.5cm}
$P(\text{fever} \mid \text{meningitis}) \approx 1$\\
\vspace{0.4cm}
\onslide<2->{
$P(\text{meningitis} \mid \text{fever}) \ll 1$ \\
\scriptsize{(fever is often due to minor pathologies)}
}
\column{0.5\textwidth}
\begin{figure}
\includegraphics[width=0.6\textwidth]{Figures/high_fever.png}
\end{figure}
\end{columns}
```

## These Are Not the Same Thing

$$P(A \mid B) \neq P(B \mid A)$$

```{=latex}
In testing:\\
\vspace{0.5cm}
$P(\text{low score} \mid \text{dementia}) \approx $ Sensitivity\\
\vspace{0.4cm} \pause
$P(\text{dementia} \mid \text{low score}) = $ what the clinician wants \\
\pause $\to$ requires \textbf{Bayes' theorem}.
```

## Bayes' Theorem (Simply Stated)

$$P(\text{condition} \mid \text{positive}) = \frac{P(\text{positive} \mid \text{condition}) \times P(\text{condition})}{P(\text{positive})}$$

or:

\begin{center}
$$Posterior = \frac{Likelihood  \times Prior}{Evidence}$$
\end{center}

```{=latex}
\pause
\vfill
\small
\begin{itemize}
\item The \textbf{posterior} is our updated probability after seeing the result.
\item The \textbf{prior} is what we believed before the test (in PPV is the \textbf{Prevalence}).\\
\item The \textbf{likelihood} is provided by sensitivity.\\
\item The \textbf{marginal probability} is the probability of having a positive result.
\end{itemize}
```

## What is our probability?

$$P(\text{condition present} \mid \text{positive test}) = \;?$$


## Prior Probability in Clinical Practice
PPV, uses Prevalence that assume the patient is a \textbf{random draw from the population}: which is rarely true.

The relevant prior is the \textbf{pre-test probability} in the specific clinical context:

\begin{itemize}
\item A student participating to a study on OCD for additional credits $\Rightarrow$ low prior.
\item A patient referred to a clinic specialized in OCD from another specialist $\Rightarrow$ much higher prior.
\end{itemize}

## Estimating the Prior

Published prevalence figures may not match either context of the previous slide.
\pause

Existing data (e.g., interview, anamnesis, etc.) may also change the prior probability. 
\pause

In practice, the clinician's \textbf{clinical judgment} (not a published prevalence) often serves as the **prior** probability. 

## Expliciting the prior?

In clinical practice, the prior rarely takes the form of an explicit number. Clinicians typically reason with **qualitative, implicit beliefs**, a sense of how likely a condition is, shaped by experience, context, and available information (E.g. Elstein \& Schwarz, 2002)


## From probabilities to Likelihood ratios

Alternative approaches overcome this limitation (e.g. based on Likelihood ratios) based on _shifts in odds_, but they are rarely used in psychological or neuropsychological practice and so they will not be discussed in this course.

\href{https://clinicalproblemsolving.com/2020/02/09/clinical-reasoning-corner-likelihood-ratios/}{\underline{\textbf{Likelihood Ratios plainly explained}}}

## A different perspective

All these considerations tells us (in a bit formal way) a crucial aspect.
\pause

\textcolor{red}{\textbf{Test results does not have always the same meaning}}

\small
\emph{it depends on what we now about the individual we are assessing (or, in Bayesian terms, on prior probability)}


## Summary

\begin{itemize}
\item Sensitivity and specificity estimate $P(\text{result} \mid \text{condition})$, \textbf{not} $P(\text{condition} \mid \text{result})$.
\item PPV/NPV or Bayes' theorem allow to respond to this but it requires a prior (pre-test probability or \emph{base rate}).
\item Every test result is an \textbf{update of belief}, not a binary decision.
\item Good clinical assessment integrates test scores with interview, anamnesis, and collateral information as a \textbf{progressive accumulation of evidence}.
\end{itemize}

## Take-home message
![](Figures/triangle.png){width="10%" fig-align="center"}

Exact posterior probabilities are rarely calculable in clinical practice, as they require information that is seldom available: most notably the prior probability. For this reason, test results are better understood as **evidence** to be integrated with all other available information.
